data.title: SPE: A Review Of The Special Opportunities Fund Semi-Annual Report
data.description:
The Special Opportunities Fund (NYSE:SPE) recently published their semi-annual shareholder report. They reviewed the first half 2025 market performance of 10.62% which was more than 400 basis points above the S&P 500 performance of 6.20%. The market outperformance was helped by a narrowing in the discount from 11.17% on December 31, 2024 to 9.00% on June 30, 2025.

SPE has an accretive share repurchase program in place, but the repurchased quantities can vary a lot with the SPE discount. Since April 2023, they repurchased 834,810 common shares at double-digit discounts, and 99,179 shares of the Series C preferred stock below book value. But in the first eight months of 2025, there was only one repurchase of 5,000 common shares. This was mainly because the discount stayed below 10% for most of this period.

Fund Shares Outstanding, Repurchases & Conversions

Phil Goldstein provided some commentary on the first half of 2025-

The fund does not have much exposure to the stocks in the S&P 500, and its portfolio is less volatile than the index. They were able to keep up with (and exceed) the S&P 500 performance largely due to some successful activist campaigns conducted by Bulldog Investors or other activists like Saba Capital.

The IPO market for SPACs started to pick up during the first half of 2025. The percentage allocation to SPACs stayed relatively flat at 20.4% on June 30, 2025, down slightly from 20.9% at the end of 2024.

There were less good opportunities to buy deeply discounted closed-end funds, although there are still a few larger holdings of some CEFs in the portfolio. More BDCs are trading at sizable discounts to NAV for various reasons- interest rate compression, tariff uncertainty, and more competition from new players in the private loan business. Because of the large discounts in some BDCs, they anticipate more accretive share purchases and tender offers and possibly more activism or corporate actions.

The Fund owns some term trusts or other CEFs that will either liquidate or provide a liquidity event if certain conditions are not met. This provides a tailwind since any current discount will eventually be reduced or eliminated. In 2023, a settlement was reached with MFS High Yield Municipal Trust (CMU) and MFS Investment Grade Municipal Trust (CXH). Each fund committed to providing a liquidity event, unless the discount fell to no more than 7.5% by mid-2025. In July, the termination date of the settlement agreement was extended to the end of 2025. A high percentage of the outstanding shares of each fund are held by value-oriented shareholders. Phil thinks the management may likely announce a plan to eliminate the discounts before the 2026 annual meeting.

Phil Goldstein commented about a successful activist campaign with BNY Mellon Municipal Income (DMF) to get them to monetize shares at a price close to NAV. In June 2025, DMF merged into an open-end fund, effectively eliminating the discount on the shares. The shares were trading at a double-digit discount before Bulldog launched the proxy contest. SPE redeemed these shares at NAV.

Before the Ukraine war, The Central & Eastern Europe Fund (CEE) invested more than 50% of its portfolio in Russian securities. On March 14, 2022, because of US sanctions, the fund decided to value almost all of the Russian securities at zero. SPE started buying CEE at double digit discounts from the revised "zero-Russia" NAV. In the spring of 2025, they exited the entire position when CEE traded at a premium because some investors became optimistic that the war might end soon.

SPE has owned a number of BDCs that have performed well. One of the largest long-term holdings is CION Investment Corp. (CION). CION’s discount to NAV has come down from over 30% but is still high at around 26%. CION’s market yield is now about 14% per annum and it has adopted an accretive share repurchase program. (LRFC) and (PTMN) finally merged, and the name changed on August 22 to BCP Investment (BCIC). Before agreeing to vote for the merger, some large shareholders of LRFC, negotiated for significant deal enhancements. Bulldog believes the new BCIC shareholders will benefit from its larger scale, especially a commitment by BCIC and its affiliates to purchase up to 20% of its outstanding common stock if its shares trade below 80% NAV.

The largest SPE investment is an operating company, Texas Pacific Land (TPL). It is a profitable asset-rich company that owns land in West Texas, primarily in the Permian Basin. Earnings mainly consist of royalties from oil and gas producers. TPL traded as high as $1,400 during the first half of the year, due to the prospect of housing massive AI data centers. But more recently, the stock has fallen to $900 after DeepSeek, a Chinese startup, announced a low cost AI model. Bulldog believes the TPL shares are currently undervalued.

SPE also owns another real estate company- Tejon Ranch Co. (TRC). Its main asset is a 270,000-acre ranch in California. TRC generates substantial income from its Commerce Center, a fully operational commercial and industrial real estate development located near Interstate 5, on the west coast. TRC also has three master planned communities (MPCs) still in various stages of entitlement. Bulldog believes Tejon's shares have been significantly undervalued by the stock market because of the capital-intensive nature of the “MPC” assets, and an absence of current revenue or operating income from them. In order to enhance shareholder value, Bulldog Investors launched a proxy contest for board seats at Tejon’s annual meeting on May 19, 2025. This resulted in Andy Dakos, a partner in Bulldog Investors, being elected as a director. Tejon Ranch Co. is an important core holding for SPE, and Bulldog is optimistic valuations will improve with Andy on the Board.

As predicted in the last SPE annual report, activist pressure has arrived at Cannae Holdings (CNNE). It is a holding company, similar to a CEF. CNNE shares currently trade at a discount of about 33% below its “sum of the parts.”

Last year, Bulldog submitted a shareholder proposal to have CNNE hire an investment banker to study options to maximize the value of its shares. Another large shareholder announced plans to solicit proxies at the next annual meeting to elect its nominees as directors. The activism seems to be having an effect, as CNNE announced several changes in a recent press release.

SPE Portfolio Changes Between December 31, 2024 and June 30, 2025

I went through the year-end and mid-year SPE portfolios in detail, and found many changes. I've summarized the larger changes below:

I. Closed-End Fund Portfolio Changes

A. Closed-End Funds: Large Sells or Trims

BNY Sold 621,787 shares.
CEE Sold 188,883 shares when premium ran up in February.
ENX Trimmed position from 386,827 shares to 307,505.
CUBA Sold 147,693 shares. Fund ticker changed to HERZ.
NBH Sold 114,296 shares.
Platinum Asia Inv Trimmed from 2,548,150 shares to 2,220,947. Australia CEF.
BRW Sold 75,230 shares when discount narrowed.
SWZ Sold 236,992 shares. Bulldog has taken over the fund.
TYG Trimmed from 77,138 shares to 20,600.
TPZ Sold 220,215 shares. 3-way fund merger (TPZ,TTP,NDP).
Tribeca Global Natural Resources. Sold 494,484. Australia CEF.
ZTR Trimmed from 199,007 to 135,422. Discount narrowed.

B) Closed-end Funds: Large New Positions or Adds

BIGZ Increased shares from 122,923 to 305,194. Fund ticker changed to BTX.
DMB New position. 71.032 shares.
DSM Increased shares from 970,821 -> 1,263,605.
LEO New position. 224,070 shares.
GDL Increased shares from 8,457 -> 171,677. High discount with high expenses. Potential for future activism?
JOF New position. 43,871 shares.
CXE New position. 233,756 shares. High discount.
HIE Increased shares from 8,331 -> 132,868. Term fund.
NBXG Increased shares from 338,578 -> 636,454.
GF Increased shares from 268,348 -> 385,918.
MAV New position 65,545 shares. Was liquidated in August, 2025.
MHI New position 38,422 shares. Was liquidated in August, 2025.
SWZ New position. 106,806 shares. Fund changed from Swiss equity fund to hedge fund style CEF.
IHD New position 18,818 shares. Saba Capital is involved.

The total invested in Closed-end funds decreased from $94,221,235 to $92,857,385 during the first half year.

II. BDC Portfolio Changes

BBDC New position. 11,631 shares.
OTF New position 26,627 shares
CCAP New position 99,489 shares
ICMB New position 115,947 shares
RWAY Increased shares from 397,932 -> 453,850
SSSS Increased shares from 134,851 -> 177,650

III. Other Stocks/Preferred Portfolio Changes

TRC Increased shares from 400,000 -> 500,000
SEG Sold 38,155 shares.
CNNE Increased shares from 269,631 -> 380,987
CDR-C

New position 30,610 shares.

Seeking Alpha Quant Ratings For Some Large Equity Holdings

The Seeking alpha quant Model does not cover all of the equity holdings in SPE. But I've listed the overall quant ratings for some of the top holdings below followed by the Profitability sub-rating. Note that the Seeking alpha quant model gives a low profitability score for TRC and CNNE which are discounted asset value plays, but have low profitability currently.

CION Hold. 3.48 Profitability B+

TRC Hold. 2.84 Profitability F

CNNE Sell. 2.39 Profitability F

RWAY Hold. 3.25 Profitability A-

SPE- Three Year Discount History

Special Opportunities Fund, Inc.

  • Pays Managed Distribution- 8% NAV

  • Total Investment Exposure= $239 Million

  • Total Common Assets= $183 Million

  • Last Monthly Distribution= $0.1098

  • Annual Distribution Rate= 8.46%

  • Baseline Expense ratio= 1.86%

  • Discount to NAV= -9.58%

  • Average Daily Volume= 30,000

  • Average Dollar Volume= $475,000

  • Effective Leverage: 23.4% (from SPE preferred)

Potential Risks To Investing in SPE

Overall, I believe that has lower risk than an investment in the S&P 500. It has a mix of eclectic investments including an allocation to very low risk pre-merger SPACs. Morningstar puts the fund in the Moderate Target Risk category. But there are some portions of the portfolio with potentially higher risk:

  • BDCs which own some distressed securities
  • Closed-end funds can occasionally have widening discounts in times of financial stress.
  • The activist approach can lead to higher expenses due to litigation and proxy battles.
  • The fund has exposure to small and mid cap companies and foreign securities.
  • Low trading liquidity: The average trading volume is fairly low.

Summary

Overall, SPE has been performing fairly well lately, and some of their activist activities have paid off. The fund has also benefited from Saba Capital activism since they sometimes hold the same positions.

I generally like to add shares of SPE at about a 12% discount or greater. So at the current time it is more of a hold than a buy.

I have also owned the (SPE-C) preferred stock which has less upside, but in the worst case scenario, it can be redeemed for $25.04 on January 21, 2027. But the bid-asked spread for SPE-C is very wide and it no longer as much of a value as it was last year.

The SPE convertible is still "out of the money", but if SPE continues to perform well over the next year or two, the convertible feature of SPE-C may be worth something, and SPE-C could trade at a premium well above $25. It is currently convertible into 1.5407 shares of SPE. The conversion ratio increases slightly every time SPE pays out a monthly distribution.

data.title: S&P 500 Snapshot: Index Finishes The Week At Record High
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

The S&P 500 finished the week at a new record high, its 27th of the year. The index posted a weekly gain of 1.2%, marking its sixth in the past seven weeks. Here is a snapshot of the index from the past week:

The table below summarizes the number of record highs reached each year dating back to 2013.

Here is a snapshot of the index from the past six months with a 50-day moving average:

S&P 500: A Perspective on Drawdowns

On October 9, 2007, the S&P 500 reached an all-time high, closing the day at 1565.15. Then on March 9, 2009, the index dropped ~57% off of its high from exactly 17 months before, closing the day at 676.53. This time period became known as the Global Financial Crisis. It took over 5 years before the index reached a new all-time high on March 28, 2013, where it closed out at 1569.19. The chart below is a snapshot of record highs and selloffs since the 2007 peak reached on October 9, 2007.

What happens if we take out the Global Financial Crisis? Here's a snapshot of the same chart above where the start date has been changed to the trough reached on March 9, 2009. Note the recent sell-offs in 2022.

Here are a few tables with the number of days of a 1% or greater change in either direction and the number of days of corrections (down 10% or more from the record high).

And here is a linear chart of the index since October 9, 2007:

Here is a linearly scaled version of the same chart with the 50- and 200-day moving averages. The index has been above the 50-day moving average since May 1st and above the 200-day moving average since May 12th. Additionally, the 50-day moving average has been above the 200-day moving average since July 1st.

S&P 500: A Perspective on Volatility

For a sense of the correlation between the closing price and intraday volatility, the chart below overlays the S&P 500 since 2007 with the intraday price range. On April 9th, the index experienced its largest intraday price volatility (10.77%) since December 24th, 2018 (19.10%). Also included is the 20-day moving average to identify trends in volatility. Over the past 20 days, the average percent change from the intraday low to the intraday high is 0.65%.

S&P 500 versus S&P Equal Weight

The S&P 500 is a market cap-weighted index which includes roughly the 500 largest U.S. stocks spanning 11 sectors. The S&P 500 Equal Weight Index includes the same constituents as the S&P 500, but each company is equally weighted at a fixed weight. So how do these two indexes match up against each other this year?

The S&P 500 is currently up 13.56% year-to-date, while the S&P Equal Weight is up 7.91% year-to-date.

ETFs associated with the S&P 500 include: iShares Core S&P 500 ETF (IVV), SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY), Vanguard S&P 500 ETF (VOO), SPDR Portfolio S&P 500 ETF (SPLG), and Invesco S&P 500® Equal Weight ETF (RSP).

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Goldman Sachs Large Cap Equity Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Overview

The S&P 500 Index increased by 10.94% (total return, in USD) in the second quarter of 2025, whereas the Russell 2000 Index increased by 8.50% (total return, in USD). Despite an initial selloff prompted by concern regarding new US tariff policies, the market surged this quarter, supported by progress in trade negotiations, a resilient labor market, strong corporate earnings results, and better-than-expected inflationary data. Specifically, easing trade tensions and renewed investor optimism toward the artificial intelligence growth theme served as key tailwinds, contributing to the S&P 500 Index reaching all-time highs during the period. Furthermore, a series of better-than-expected Core Consumer Price Index reports, combined with resilient consumer spending trends, underpinned the strength of the US economy. The best performing sectors within the S&P 500 were Information Technology, Communication Services, and Industrials, while the worst performing sectors were Energy, Health Care, and Real Estate. For the Russell 2000, the best performing sectors were Information Technology, Industrials, and Materials, while the worst performing sectors were Real Estate, Utilities, and Consumer Staples.

Portfolio Attribution

The Goldman Sachs Large Cap Equity Fund (MUTF:GSCGX) outperformed its benchmark, the Russell 1000 Index (net), during the quarter. The Industrials and Consumer Discretionary sectors contributed to returns, while the Information Technology and Materials sectors detracted from returns.

Our position in energy equipment manufacturing and services company, GE Vernova Inc. (GEV)(0.8%), was a top contributor to relative returns during the quarter. The company is a leading global supplier of gas turbines (gas power generation), wind turbines (primarily onshore with leading US market share), nuclear (JV with Hitachi for small modular reactors) and various other power equipment offerings. The company’s stock price continued to rise over the past several months as the market maintains confidence in GE Vernova’s management team’s ability to execute operationally and increase order quantities to scale alongside the artificial intelligence growth narrative. Furthermore, the company’s strong balance sheet and history of outperformance further underpinned this positive momentum. Overall, we remain positive on not only the company’s growth opportunities in its Power and Electrification verticals, but also on the trajectory of the margins over the next several years. Bolstered by positive tailwinds of the growing demand for US power onshoring, we believe that GE Vernova may continue to outpace peers in the future.

Performance History as of 6/30/2025

For periods one year or greater, performance is annualized. The returns represent past performance. Past performance does not guarantee future results. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance quoted above. Please visit am.gs.com to obtain the most recent month-end returns. Performance reflects cumulative total returns for periods of less than one year and average annual total returns for periods of greater than one year. All Fund performance data reflect the reinvestment of distributions.

Pursuant to a contractual arrangement, the Fund’s waivers and/or expense limitations will remain in place through at least December 29, 2025, and prior to such date the investment adviser may not terminate the arrangements without the approval of the Fund’s Board of Trustees. Please refer to the Fund’s prospectus for the most recent expenses.

Our position in the global cruise company, Royal Caribbean Cruises Ltd. (RCL)(0.8%), was a top contributor to relative returns during the second quarter. The company’s stock price rose following a positive first quarter corporate earnings release, influenced by robust demand and favorable pricing, particularly across European markets. The introduction of new ships and expanded initiatives, like the River Cruise, were a catalyst for the company's strides towards growth. These company initiatives, combined with the company's strong fundamentals and disciplined capacity management, supports a positive outlook for its continued financial performance. We continue to believe that Royal Caribbean has a long runway for growth and that the company trades at a discount to intrinsic value given solid underlying macroeconomic and company-specific trends.

Broadcom Inc. (AVGO)(1.9%), a designer, developer, manufacturer, and global supplier of semiconductor and infrastructure software products, was a top detractor from relative returns during the quarter due to our relative underweight position. The company’s stock price rose on the back of increased investor confidence following management’s announcement of a $10 billion share repurchase program, growing artificial intelligence infrastructure demand, and optimistic earnings expectations. Further contributing to the stock’s performance during the quarter was Broadcom’s strong fiscal second quarter earnings results in June. Management provided increased visibility on its artificial intelligence semiconductor revenue growth, with the majority of growth being driven by AI Application Specific Integrated Circuits (ASIC) going forward. As the leading supplier of artificial intelligence (AI) Application Specific Integrated Circuits (ASIC) accelerators, we continue to believe Broadcom is well positioned to benefit from the accelerated demand from ASIC chips. Whereas GPUs are specifically designed to run multiple workloads through parallel processing, ASICs, which were purposefully built for a specific workload, can perform that task much more efficiently and at significantly lower cost. We continue to have a positive outlook on Broadcom as its non-AI business has seen stability and our view of the size of the AI ASIC market over time has become more bullish, though we are maintaining our underweight position as we balance our investments across the broader semiconductor industry.

Enterprise software company, Oracle Corporation (ORCL)(0.2%), was a top detractor from relative returns during the quarter due to our relative underweight position in the name. The company’s stock price rose throughout the quarter on the back of improving investor sentiment for cloud infrastructure and artificial intelligence. Furthermore, Oracle reported strong earnings results in June, with rising cloud revenue growth and delivering a positive outlook. We maintain our belief in the company as robust growth within future contracts and large deal booking illuminates a promising path forward. Overall, we believe the company is well-positioned to benefit from increasing demand for artificial intelligence and will reap long-term benefits as it continues to tactfully navigate and innovate within the evolving space.

Fund holdings and allocations shown are unaudited, and may not be representative of current or future investments. Fund holdings and allocations may not include the Fund’s entire investment portfolio, which may change at any time. Fund holdings should not be relied on in making investment decisions and should not be construed as research or investment advice regarding particular securities. Current and future holdings are subject to risk.

Portfolio Review

We initiated a position in Capital One Financial Corporation (COF)(0.8%), a commercial banking service provider, during the quarter. We are optimistic about the company following the development of a fintech-oriented management team, which should facilitate smooth technological integration with Discover Financial Services. The company is strategically focused on constructing a comprehensive digital banking platform by refining debit card programs, refining risk management protocols, and enhancing network capabilities. This multi-faceted approach, coupled with the drive to expand Discover's reach and leverage synergies to develop a robust national bank, positions Capital One for long-term growth.

We initiated a position in the aftermarket automotive parts and accessories retailer, AutoZone, Inc. (AZO)(0.6%), during the quarter. We believe that the company may benefit from secular tailwinds as inflation rates, the aging car population, and favorable car market dynamics may work to grow the industry further. Furthermore, we believe that the company is poised to increase market share through its strategic initiative to accelerate deployment of its hubs, with the aim of increasing network productivity and footprint. Ultimately, we maintain confidence in the company as its management team has outlined several growth opportunities that we believe may prove beneficial in the long run, leading us to initiate a position in the name.

We exited our position in Johnson & Johnson (JNJ)(0.0%), a multinational pharmaceutical, biotechnology, and medical technologies corporation. We like the name given. The decision was primarily driven by concerns that macroeconomic factors like regulatory changes and tariffs could negatively impact business units within the company. While we continue to like and monitor the name, we ultimately exited our position in favor of better risk/reward in the portfolio.

We exited our position in diversified financial services company, Wells Fargo & Company (WFC)(0.0%), during the quarter. We believe that there are several potential headwinds to the company’s net interest income that may pressure the stock in the future. Specifically, we have lower future expectations for the company’s commercial loan growth and mortgage vertical and concerns regarding increased commercial deposit competition. Due to our slightly dampened outlook, this recent period of elevated stock price valuation provided an attractive opportunity for us to exit our position. While we will continue to monitor the name, we decided to exit and reallocate to positions that we have stronger forward conviction for.

Past performance does not guarantee future results, which may vary. Fund holdings and allocations shown are unaudited, and may not be representative of current or future investments. Fund holdings and allocations may not include the Fund's entire investment portfolio, which may change at any time. Fund holdings should not be relied on in making investment decisions and should not be construed as research or investment advice regarding particular securities. Current and future holdings are subject to risk.

Strategy/Outlook

The second quarter was characterized by themes of resilience as the US equity market recovered and outperformed from the previous quarter’s weakness, as strong quarterly earnings and the improvement of newly implemented trade policy positioning led to a resurgence in investor optimism. As we traverse ever-changing market conditions, we believe active management is critical to navigating economic uncertainties, in pursuit of providing diversified sources of returns. Unlike passive management strategies, which may carry exposure to low-quality constituents, taking an active approach may enable investors to avoid potential pitfalls and adapt to shifting macroeconomic conditions and geopolitical backdrop. As we navigate this period of heightened volatility, we remain nimble and look to capitalize on idiosyncratic opportunities uncovered through bottom-up stock selection. We continue to prioritize our quality-oriented approach to investing by focusing on having a long-term viewpoint on the portfolio, seeking businesses with healthy balance sheets, and partnering with management teams that are effective stewards of capital. In our view, we are optimistic that a fundamental approach may generate excess returns in the long run for our clients.

Source: Goldman Sachs Asset Management, FactSet as of 6/30/2025.

Risk Considerations

Effective April 30, 2025, the Goldman Sachs Large Cap Core Fund was renamed the Goldman Sachs Large Cap Equity Fund. Through February 11, 2022, certain of the Fund’s principal investment strategies differed. Performance information prior to this date reflects the Fund’s former strategies.

The Goldman Sachs Large Cap Equity Fund invests primarily in U.S. equity investments, and will invest in both value and growth companies. The Fund’s investments are subject to market risk, which means that the value of the securities in which it invests may go up or down in response to the prospects of individual companies, particular sectors or governments and/or general economic conditions. Foreign and emerging markets investments may be more volatile and less liquid than investments in U.S. securities and are subject to the risks of currency fluctuations and adverse economic, social or political developments. Different investment styles(e.g., “growth”) tend to shift in and out of favor, and at times the Fund may underperform other funds that invest in similar asset classes.

General Disclosures

It includes those Russell 1000 Index companies with lower price-to-book ratios and lower expected growth values. This index is constructed to provide a comprehensive and unbiased barometer for the large-cap value segment. The Index is completely reconstituted annually to ensure new and growing equities are included and that the represented companies continue to reflect value characteristics. It is not possible to invest directly in an unmanaged index.

The S&P 500 Index is an unmanaged index of 500 stocks that is generally representative of the performance of larger companies in the U.S. Please note an investor cannot invest directly in an index. It is not possible to invest directly in an unmanaged index.

This information may not be current and Goldman Sachs Asset Management has no obligation to provide any updates or changes.

Definitions:

Magnificent Seven: seven mega-cap technology companies characterized by their substantial market capitalizations, dominant positions in their respective industries, and significant influence on the overall economy. Constituents include AAPL, MSFT, AMZN, NVDA, GOOG, GOOGL, META, TSLA.

Views and opinions expressed are for informational purposes only and do not constitute a recommendation by Goldman Sachs Asset Management to buy, sell, or hold any security. Views and opinions are current as of the date of this presentation and may be subject to change, they should not be construed as investment advice.

Goldman Sachs & Co. LLC is the distributor of the Goldman Sachs Funds.

© 2025 Goldman Sachs. All rights reserved.

Date of first use: 8/5/2025

Compliance Code: 447122-OTU

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: lululemon: Don't Be Tempted
data.description:
My bearish rating on lululemon athletica Inc. (NASDAQ:LULU)(NEOE:LULU:CA) in February has definitely been one of my better calls this year so far. After stating that the stock could be a lemon, lululemon has plunged around 55%, and recent post-earnings declines have been punishing. I believe now is a great time to provide an update for this well known brand. In the below analysis, many bearish signs are shown. Sales growth is slowing, profitability is getting eroded, and forward guidance is weak. With that in mind, the valuation seems rightfully low, and I have decided to reiterate my sell rating due to a deteriorating fundamental outlook.

Sales Analysis

lululemon reported their 2025 Q2 results just earlier this month, and top line growth is pointed in the wrong direction, indicating that demand is continuing to soften. The company saw net revenues of $2.525 billion, up 6.5% YoY. As you can see above, this growth rate is the lowest of the past three years, and so investors should definitely be concerned with the degree of slowdown that they are witnessing. lululemon also missed expectations by $14.88 million, and so there isn't much to celebrate here. When breaking down their revenues by geographic region, International sales were actually quite strong as there was 22% growth. On the other hand, the Americas saw just a 1% increase, likely showing that tariff headwinds have taken a toll on consumers. With the Americas being lululemon's largest and thus most important market, this relative weakness should raise some concerns.

For comparable sales, top line results were arguably even more concerning in my view. Total comparable sales increased by just 1% YoY. However, in Q1 the growth rate was also just 1%. In geographic terms, there is also a major difference between the Americas and the international market. Growth was -4% for the former and +15% for the latter and so once again, tariffs seem to biting into lululemon's key market. CFO Meghan Frank stated that they're "navigating industry-wide challenges" and while I do agree with that statement, it does seem that lululemon is getting hit particularly hard. Moving forward, management should focus on reigniting growth in the Americas or at least stopping the declines. They can potentially achieve this through innovative designs or fresh marketing campaigns.

Inventory Turnover Is Worrying

As of August 3, 2025, lululemon had a total of $1.723 billion in inventories. That is a 20.54% increase YoY and a 19.45% QoQ increase. With sales growth slowing, this rise in inventories may be a concern. Therefore, I believe it would be worthwhile to calculate their inventory turnover to see if products are piling up. To calculate inventory turnover for Q2, we'll divide Q2 COGS by average inventory. Average inventory is calculated using the figures at the end of Q1 and Q2. Inventory turnover comes out to be 0.66. That is quite a low inventory turnover and so investors should definitely be concerned about how slow the company has been able to convert merchandise into revenues. Moving forward, the company may have to have more frequent or deeper sales to try to clear out this old inventory, which will potentially mean margin erosion. Their sales are usually called "We Made Too Much," and it certainly seems that name applies now. To avoid such issues from happening in the future, the company needs to better assess the macro-environment and be more prudent over the quantity they are producing. This will help with product margins and eliminate costs associated with overstocking.

Margins Contract

Speaking of margins, there are already clear signs that the company is indeed having to sell merchandise for less than they expected. For gross margin, they saw a 110 basis point contraction to 58.5% and so obviously costs as a percentage of revenues have gone up. However, the company's profitability drop is beyond just costs. Expenses increased at a faster rate than the top line to indicate deteriorating corporate efficiency. YoY, SG&A expenses increased from being 36.8% of revenues to being 37.7% of revenues. With that mind, lululemon's operating margin contracted by 210 basis points to 20.7%. While that's still not a bad margin, the contraction does show that perhaps the overstocking of inventories is beginning to take a toll on the company. Furthermore, management should have plans on how to streamline their corporate operations as well in order to boost efficiency. This may include the need for downsizing, but perhaps restructuring would be an appropriate first step for the company.

The Bottom Line

With top line growth slowing and both gross and operating margins contracting, expectations should be low for the bottom line. lululemon reported a diluted EPS of $3.10, down 1.59% YoY. Back in Q1, growth wasn't great, but at least it was positive at 2.36%. Still, the company was able to beat expectations in Q2 by a quite nice margin of $0.24. Therefore, analysts were expecting even worse profitability than what they reported. I'll have a bit more to say on their outlook below but according to consensus estimates, things may get worse before they get better. As you can see above, EPS growth is expected to be significantly negative in the next four quarters. That is an ominous sign for lululemon. Personally, I find this outlook to be unsurprising, but given that the Fed now seems to be more willing to cut rates, there is potential that consumer confidence will return quicker than expected and render these estimates overly pessimistic.

Q3 & Full Year Guidance

lululemon provided guidance for both Q3 and 2025 as a whole. Let's start with their annual outlook. Firstly, for net revenues, the company lowered their range from $11.150 billion - $11.300 billion to $10.850 billion - $11.000 billion. This new range represents growth of 4% to 6% when excluding the 53rd week of 2024. This is compared with 10% of growth in full year 2024 and so there are clear signs of deceleration even in the longer time frame. Their EPS range was also lowered from $14.58 - $14.78 to $12.77 - $12.97. At the midpoint, this new guidance represents a 12.09% decline YoY. Clearly, that is a very concerning projection and shows that profitability is under a lot of pressure. Now let's move on and see what they're expecting for Q3 specifically.

For Q3, lululemon sees revenues in the range of $2.47 billion to $2.50 billion, and that represents growth of between 3% and 4%. Therefore, top line growth is expected to set a fresh multiyear low after new lows in Q2. As for EPS, they are projecting $2.18 to $2.23. That implies a 23.17% decline from the year ago quarter. This is around in-line with what consensus estimates are calling for and so I believe it is clear that the near term outlook of the company is alarming. Again, as stated in the EPS section, the near term doesn't look great now but Fed rate cuts from now until the end of the year may be able to boost sentiment and result in better performance in consumer discretionary companies.

Valuation Contraction Justified

lululemon's forward P/E ratio has seen major contraction from the highs of earlier this year. The multiple is now just at 12.64 but I would be hesitant to call the stock a bargain. Currently, there is honestly not much going right for the company. Sales growth is decelerating, inventory is piling up, and profitability is deteriorating. Guidance and consensus estimates suggest that these unfavorable trends are likely to continue and may get even worse from here. With that being the case, I believe the forward P/E ratio deserves to be at multiyear lows. Recall that revenue growth is likely to set another new multiyear low in Q3 and so it should not be that surprising to see the company's valuation in a depressed state. In my view, there doesn't seem to be many positive catalysts to help stop this bad run unless rate cuts cause a notable increase in consumer confidence. KeyBanc expressed a similar view just very recently and so I would have to say that despite the low valuation, lululemon is more than likely not a bargain at this point.

Technical Analysis

In terms of the long term, the 50 day SMA and 200 day SMA had a death cross back in late April. In hindsight, investors should have gotten out back then. Currently, the gap between the two SMAs is continuing to expand and so therefore bearish momentum is picking up steam at this point. As for the near term, the technicals are still very much bearish but there may be an opportunity for traders to play a bounce. The 9 day EMA is currently significantly below the 21 day EMA and the stock is also underneath the Bollinger Bands midline after breaking below earlier this month. The MACD remains in a net bearish position currently and the RSI reading of 31.04 shows very strong control of the stock by the bears. However, notice the positive divergence shown by both the MACD and RSI. For the MACD, the low this month has held far above the June low and for the RSI, the low this month has held notably above the lows of August. Therefore, there is potential for a bullish reversal and traders could play that even if it's just a dead cat bounce.

Takeaway

lululemon has had a brutal selloff in 2025 and in September. While the worst of the declines may be behind us, I believe there may still be more pain for the stock. As stated earlier, Q3 was a weak quarter on many measures and financial results are likely going to deteriorate further before they get better. Without clear positive catalysts on the horizon, the stock's valuation contraction seems like a rational reaction to a weakening business. Even though the valuation multiple is fair, if the EPS declines continue indefinitely, the stock may have room to fall further. Therefore, I have decided to maintain my sell rating on lululemon as a thorough analysis shows that the stock is not a bargain even at current levels.

data.title: United Microelectronics: Cyclicality, Structure, And Dividends Assessed
data.description:
Investment thesis

Investors aren’t showing much love for United Microelectronics Corporation (NYSE:UMC) at the moment. The only Seeking Alpha analyst rating in the past 90 days is a Sell. The Quant system gives it a Hold, and the Wall Street analysts have one Strong Buy, two Holds, one Sell, and one Strong Sell.

To assess its current condition, I analyzed cyclicality in the semiconductor industry, UMC's evolving structural strategy, and its dividends. My conclusion: a Sell rating.

Cyclicality

United Microelectronics focuses on mature products, and it looks for markets with long lifecycles, including computers, smartphones, and the auto industry.

We will compare it with three other semiconductor firms: GlobalFoundries Inc. (GFS), ON Semiconductor (ON), and Skyworks Solutions, Inc. (SWKS).

UMC reported in its 20-F for 2024 that the semiconductor industry is highly cyclical and seasonal. As a result, orders and revenues are highly variable

On the seasonal side, it stated that lows usually occur in the first half of the year, because of inventory corrections by its customers.

GFS also reports that cyclicality and seasonality are important issues in the industry. It pointed out in its 20-F for 2024 that the industry suffers from periods of overcapacity.

The effects of that cyclicality can be seen in this chart of UMC, GFS, ON, and SWKS revenues over the past decade:

All four companies have similar revenue profiles, indicating that UMC’s top-line troubles are the result of industry cyclicality, rather than self-inflicted.

That idea is backed up by a scan of the four firms’ profitability, their net income margins:

From a shareholder perspective, this boils down to returns on equity:

Again, all four players have similar profiles, making the case that UMC’s fortunes are being substantially affected by industry conditions rather than something it has done or not done.

What’s the current state of the cycle? According to the Deloitte Center for Technology, Media & Telecommunications, it depends on which part of the industry you’re in:

“it is worth noting that “average” chip stock performance in the last two years has been a “tale of two markets”: Companies involved in the generative AI chip market outperformed that average, while companies without that exposure (automotive, computer, smartphone, and communications semiconductor companies, for example) underperformed.”

UMC operates in the latter market, so we can’t expect any exciting new growth in at least the near future. According to this report, computer sales are expected to grow by about 4% in 2025 and smartphone sales by 6.2%. That suggests the current upswing in the industry won’t make UMC a growth stock anytime soon.

Summing up, a change in the cycle won’t do much to improve UMC’s short-term outlook. But, if markets work as they usually do, there is likely more promise in the long-term.

Cost and revenue structure

UMC management noted in the 20-F for 2024 that the majority of its costs and expenses are fixed or semi-fixed, so fluctuations in selling prices have had a “substantial” effect on its margins and profitability.

One way the company is dealing with this situation is by continuing to develop its 40- and 55-nanometer code. On the Q2 earnings call, Co-President Jason Wang said, “Following our rollout of the 22- and 28-nanometer technologies, we'll continue to provide specialty technology in 40- and 55- nanometer node where the percentage of our revenue contribution, competing with the pricing foundries will continue to decline.”

In response to an analyst question about next year’s pricing, Wang added

“I mean we continue working with our customers in terms of pricing compensation closely, but those are more of a tactical conversation. I think fundamentally, like I reported earlier, we -- I think our key focus is try to differentiate our technology offering and so that we can continue to enhance our product mix improve the ASP [Average Sales Price] resilience.”

Like many other technology companies, then, UMC’s strategy is to use innovation to become less price sensitive. If it can deliver on the strategy, and there is no reason to think it cannot, its margins and profitability should become more stable.

Dividends

On the UMC summary page, the Seeking Alpha system warns the company is at high risk of cutting its dividend: “The company has a Dividend Safety Score of F. Over the past 11 years 64.4% of stocks with an F rating did cut their dividend.”

In addition, recent articles by Seeking Alpha analysts argue the high dividend yield, 7.00% with the September 18 closing price at $6.99, can’t last.

Personally, when I see a yield that high, I, too, become suspicious. But, I also want to know more. First, here’s a brief history of UMC’s dividend over the past decade, while noting it is an annual dividend, paid out after the close of the second quarter (shown here in U.S. dollars):

Summing up, we see it roughly doubled in 2021, almost doubled again in 2022, and went up again in 2023, before a pullback in 2024, and another increase in 2025.

But the yield hasn’t just been driven by increases; it’s also grown because the price has fallen significantly since peaking in 2021:

There are several takeaways from its high dividend strategy, expressed most recently at its May 28 shareholders’ meeting when it increased the dividend from $0.4558 to $0.4833, a 6.0% increase.

First, it is a mature company focused on less profitable, but more predictable markets. It is not participating in the higher speed categories, which would require substantially more capital investment to stay competitive.

Second, companies often pay above average dividends to signal to investors that they are financially strong and thus able to reward shareholders generously. This will be of particular interest to institutional investors.

Third, it provides an incentive for income investors to stick with it despite a share price that has fallen and can’t seem to sustain an increasing price.

Fourth, note the difference between the price return and the total return:

Could, should, or would UMC management cut the dividend? It seems unlikely since they had an opportunity to make a cut a few months ago, but opted for that 6.0% increase.

And what would they do with the extra cash flow if they did reduce the dividend? Given its low capital intensity, at least compared with semiconductor companies at the leading edge, no major expansions of funding appear to be needed.

According to the Q2-2025 balance sheet, it had $3.831 billion in cash and cash equivalents. Since it has that much cash available, it seems the most likely use of extra cash would be to increase an already rich cash and cash equivalents account.

Alternatively, it could buy back shares, but it currently does not have an authorized repurchase program. As for paying down long-term debt, that was $1.423 billion at the end of Q2, less than half the cash available.

All these things considered, I don’t consider a dividend cut likely and, as a result, think it is relatively safe at the current level until next year’s annual meeting.

Valuation

As the price chart above showed, UMC’s share price has not been able to post sustainable gains for about four years now. As a result, it is no surprise that it gets high grades for valuation:

Using the intrinsic value calculator at OmniCalculator, we will try to quantify its value:

That’s based on 2024’s earnings per share; an average CAGR of 14.87% per year over the past 10 years, the current Aaa-rated corporate bonds rate, and the price at closing on September 15.

The result points in the same direction as the average EPS estimate of the three Wall Street analysts who follow UMC. They expect earnings to dip 22.04% for 2025, and share prices often follow earnings. As noted, they have a collective Hold, based on one Strong Buy, two Holds, one Sell, and one Strong Sell.

The Quant system also has a Hold, while the only Seeking Alpha analyst rating is a Sell.

I am downgrading my rating from a Hold in my January 2021 article to a Sell now. With earnings likely remaining depressed for the near future, the share price is more likely to go down than go up. That would lead to a capital loss that might be greater than the return provided by a 7.15% dividend.

Risk factors

My Sell rating is influenced by the idea there is currently no catalyst likely to pick up the markets in which UMC operates. That may change at any time, and if it does, the company’s earnings and share price could begin rising again.

In technology markets, innovation can be extremely powerful. For example, UMC could expand its platform or develop new technology that leapfrogs what is available from its competitors.

Through the courts or negotiations, the threat of tariffs could be lifted from this Taiwanese company, and make it more attractive to American investors.

My conclusions and those of other analysts are based on sets of assumptions. The conclusions I or they reached above may be incorrect because our premises were wrong or untimely.

The inputs I provided for the intrinsic value calculator, and particularly the “Expected annual growth rate” may be too high or too low, triggering valuations that are too high or too low.

Conclusion

The bloom is off the rose that is United Microelectronics Corporation. Its earnings, share price, EPS estimates provide no signs of coming improvement.

Mainly, this is the result of semiconductor industry cyclicality, and unfortunately there are no signs of an uptick for companies with more mature products. There may be some top and bottom line improvement if UMC can successfully execute on its plan to shift to faster products.

While opportunities for capital gains appear slim, income investors will continue to make the most of UMC’s high dividend. That’s a dividend I do not believe will be cut , if at all, until at least next spring’s annual meeting.

Overall, I have no found a convincing reason to buy or hold UMC stock, so I am rating it a Sell.

data.title: PWB: Closer But Not There Yet
data.description:
The Invesco Large Cap Growth ETF (NYSEARCA:PWB), launched on 03/03/2005 and managed by Invesco Capital Management LLC., tracks the Dynamic Large Cap Growth Intellidex Index to offer exposure to large-cap growth stocks based on a multifactor selection methodology. It charges an expense ratio of 0.55% and has $1.3 billion in AUM.

In my first article covering PWB, I said that the mid-term outlook is better than the short-term one. This is still the case because of the climate we are still in, but investors and traders may want to keep monitoring, as an opportunity may appear soon. For now, large-cap growth is still not warranted, but large caps in general seem to have more potential than they did a few months ago due to the removal of certain risks from the frame.

In this article, I want to revisit the portfolio of PWB to understand the dominant drivers behind its performance as well as the challenges its approach presents right now, but also the opportunity it can present in the near term. I will also give a recap of its performance since I last covered it, as well as talk about the risks that are still present and are the reason it is unlikely it will outperform the broad market on a risk-adjusted basis in the short term.

Portfolio

First of all, its portfolio represents lower idiosyncratic risk than your regular S&P 500 fund. The top holding, Broadcom (AVGO), accounts for only 3.9% of the portfolio. Remember that this is a growth ETF that doesn't ignore the context of other fundamental factors, so the weighting and the top selections don't reflect the simplicity that S&P 500 growth funds exhibit.

And as I noted in the first article, the tech exposure of PWB is more modest than in many large-cap growth ETFs, probably due to the more sophisticated methodology. Beyond that, the rest of the sector weights appear similar to other broad-market ETFs.

It's this kind of differentiation that can make PWB very useful in both long-term investing and tactical allocation. Investors and traders can express aggressiveness when conditions warrant it, but not fall prey to the increased volatility that simple growth strategies come with in the meantime.

Performance

But with that kind of approach comes the opportunity cost if the market is particularly confident despite the macro environment. That has been the case in the last two months, as S&P 500 growth stocks outperformed PWB by a meaningful margin. The ETF performed similarly to the broader market.

This makes sense when the pure growth factor increases the concentration towards tech stocks and a few S&P 500 names that are used as vehicles to bet on the AI narrative. ETFs like PWB must lag when the market grows more optimistic and portfolios that reflect more risk are seen as more attractive.

Another thing to note here is that PWB inflows have become more modest in these last two months, likely reflecting a waning appetite for aggressive approaches.

During the summer, it's also worth mentioning that the gap between the ETF's 10DMA and 50DMA narrowed. Volatility has expanded as a result of the strong uptrend after we entered September, but the trading action shows that it is already growing weaker.

To be fair, as of 9/18/2025, RSI has climbed to ~70 as PWB rose, reflecting the optimism of the market since the FOMC meeting concluded with a decision to lower the Federal Funds rate by 25 bps. It remains to be seen if the recent weakness observed is going to be outweighed by a renewed bullishness.

Risks

Though PWB has done well so far this month, I should mention that September has been one of the weaker months in terms of returns based on data going back 21 years.

Moreover, the current positioning in equities is not favorable at all. Fund managers (NAAIM) have reallocated to levels higher than those seen before the April drawdown, and retail investors (AAII) have slowly followed in their footsteps since that month. This crowded long positioning creates a vulnerability in case of a pullback.

The same can be said for VIX, which is at a low level, reflecting the optimism of the market, or more precisely, the lack of fear. This lack of hedges can exacerbate forced selling if and when a pullback occurs, realizing an opportunity cost for PWB holders relative to investors in more defensive options.

It's those risks that I'm more concerned about right now. Since the FOMC meeting is over, fundamentals look more promising. But due to high market valuation and the current positioning, we need to see further proof that the market appreciates the shift in fundamental risks. Until then, I am maintaining my hold rating.

Your Turn

I am always open to challenging my views because being an ideologue in investing and trading can prove expensive. So if you have a different opinion, this platform is the right place to discuss both strategy and the various options with which we can position our portfolios. Go ahead and leave a comment below to let me know what you think.

Otherwise, please let me know if you found this article useful. It means a lot!

Thank you for reading.

data.title: BDC Weekly Review: Gladstone Takes A Dive On Its Convertible Issuance
data.description:
Welcome to another installment of our BDC Market Weekly Review, where we discuss market activity in the Business Development Company [BDC] sector from both the bottom-up, highlighting individual news and events-as well as the top-down-providing an overview of the broader market.

We also try to add some historical context as well as relevant themes that look to be driving the market or that investors ought to be mindful of. This update covers the period through the second week of September.

Market Action

BDCs were down on the week, outperforming only mortgage REITs in the income market. GLAD led to the downside, something we touch on below.

Year-to-date, the sector is pretty flat on average with significant variation among individual BDCs.

The median valuation in our coverage is about halfway between the recent Liberation Day drawdown and its historic average, making the sector attractive despite the income headwinds of lower short-term rates on the horizon.

Market Themes

Gladstone Capital Corp. (GLAD) issued a convertible bond this week. The bond has a coupon of 5.875% and a maturity of 2030. The greenshoe option is $19.5 against an offering of $130m.

There are a couple of interesting aspects to the issuance that are worth parsing even for investors not interested in the company. For one, the coupon on the bond looks unusually high for a convertible bond. The reason convertible bonds are issued by companies is because they can pay a significantly lower coupon than if they issued a traditional bond. The conversion option (where the bond converts into common shares at a certain share price) has value to holders, which means they are willing to accept a much lower coupon than on a standard bond. The median coupon in the iShares Convertible Bond ETF (ICVT) is just 1.5% - a partial list is below.

Convertible bonds are popular with growth sectors like Technology companies for the simple reason that growth stocks tend to have the most upside so investors are willing to accept convertible bond coupons that are not far off zero.

BDCs are not growth companies so it is unusual for them to issue convertible bonds and the fact that the coupon on GLAD's convertible bond is not just high for a convertible bond but high for a BDC shows that the conversion option does not add much value. The longer-term GLAD price chart does not scream "growth company" and that's why it doesn't translate into a low coupon on its convertible bond. GLAD still derives some value on the issuance, it's just not very much.

The other interesting element here is that on the news of the issuance the stock fell around 10%, mostly on the day after. The traditional reason for possible common share price weakness on convertible bond issuance has to do with the potential for earnings dilution.

If the convertible bond converts into common shares, there are more shares for the same amount of earnings, meaning the EPS should fall which is then reflected in the lower share price. A less common reason for price weakness is that a convertible bond issuance is a signal by management that its share price is expensive - in effect the convertible bond issuance is the company selling out of the money calls on the stock.

The EPS argument is valid for a traditional company where earnings are more or less fixed however they are not valid for a BDC where earnings can be easily pushed higher just by increasing lending i.e. buying more income-generating assets with the new equity capital.

The likely reason the share price fell in this case was due to convertible arbitrage, where traders buy the convertible bonds and sell the common shares, looking to make profits from movement between the two. It didn’t help that GLAD was somewhat expensive and it remains so.

Market Commentary

Barings BDC (BBDC) issued a 5.2% 2028 bond. What’s interesting here is that the 5.2% yield is quite a bit below the recent run of BDC bonds. For example, GLAD issued a 5.875% 2030 bond, CSWC issued a 5.95% 2030 bond, GSBD issued a 5.65% 2030 bond, GECC issued a 7.75% 2030 bond. Only ARCC came out with a lower rate at 5.1% for its 2031 bond and only a touch. This is despite ARCC being one of the highest-regarded BDCs, trading at a 13% premium while BBDC trades at a 15% discount.

What helped BBDC achieve a low rate on the bond was that it got a bit lucky with the market where interest rates fell into its own issuance. It also issued a 3-year bond which is somewhat unusual but it does save around 0.1% in interest expense given the shape of the yield curve, all else equal. And BBDC has an investment-grade rating from Fitch and Moody’s which certainly helped.

The company’s weighted-average interest expense is right around 5.2% so the new bond will help keep it around there. There was a $50m 4.66% bond that matured in August however it’s a small part of the overall $1.5bn liability stack. Opportunistic bond refinancing is helping the sector generate a decent amount of net investment income despite the headwind of lower short-term rates.

BBDC is a fairly average BDC (its 3Y total NAV return is a touch below the average) however its discount is very wide at 17%. This means that on a valuation-adjusted basis it screens as very attractive, coming in 2.4% above the average figure in our coverage. For this reason we continue to hold the stock in our High Income Portfolio.

data.title: Embraer: Equity Story Has Gotten Structurally Stronger
data.description:
Summary

Following my coverage on Embraer (NYSE:ERJ) in Sept’22, I recommended a buy rating, as I thought the stock was significantly undervalued on an SOTP basis. Back then, I had a share price target of $35, but the stock price has performed way better than I expected, now trading at $56. I think it's time to give an update. What has changed, however, is not just the price but the entire narrative. ERJ has moved from being viewed as a stable core business with a long-term Eve option to a self-sustaining growth story with record backlog, diversified demand, and clear earnings visibility. I remain buy-rated.

Solid order and demand visibility

When I last covered ERJ in 2022, my thesis was anchored on an SOTP story that required patience, with much of the long-term value tied to its Eve stake. Fast forward to today, I think the narrative has shifted dramatically. ERJ is no longer just a stable core business with a call option embedded. I believe it has become a growth story with its own levers, and it is delivering results that the market has yet to fully appreciate. The clearest evidence of this shift is the order book. ERJ’s backlog has surged to an all-time high of US$29.7 billion, up 40% y/y. This is not just for show. This ~$30 billion basically locks in years of revenue visibility and de-risks the growth outlook. Also, the book-to-bill ratio is averaging ~2x across all business units over the past twelve months, which confirms that this is not a one-off blip but is supported by solid demand.

Within that, the Avelo Airlines deal (50 firm E195-E2 jets plus 50 options) is a major milestone, in my view. Beyond the multi-billion-dollar list value, the strategic importance here is what investors should pay attention to. To be specific, this is the first US order for the next-generation E2, and for ERJ to successfully penetrate the world’s largest aviation market, it really goes to show the aircraft’s competitiveness. More importantly, it de-risks the entire E2 program now that ERJ has a credible US reference customer. This should open the door for other carriers to adopt ERJ’s E2 aircraft as well.

Diversified Growth

When we look through the veil of ERJ’s strong backlog, it is evident that demand strength is not limited to any single segment. Take the Defense & Security, for instance. This segment has undergone a remarkable turnaround. Its backlog doubled y/y, driven by the growing global adoption of the C-390 Millennium. Portugal’s recent follow-on order—which is a significant milestone itself, as it is the first by a NATO operator—and the fact that seven NATO members have now selected the aircraft provide a powerful “validation” of ERJ’s capabilities. With NATO's commitment to spend 5% of GDP on defense, I believe this may be the start of a strong growth cycle for ERJ. Given that defense programs are typically long-duration and higher margin (for perspective, the financial impact is already visible, with segment EBIT margin inflecting from -0.5% in 2Q24 to 9.2% in 2Q25), it adds another layer of visibility into growth outlook and also stability to earnings.

Meanwhile, ERJ’s services & support segment backlog has also reached a record $4.9 billion, up 55% y/y, which clearly shows strong demand for lifecycle solutions. These after-sales services include crucial maintenance, repair, and operations [MRO], engine maintenance, and parts support for ERJ's growing global fleet. As you can imagine, this segment becomes structurally more important over time as growth is tied to ERJ’s expanding installed base. And because this is an essential demand, this segment provides a stable, recurring revenue stream that is far less cyclical than aircraft sales. More importantly, it carries significantly higher margins (28.8% gross margin in 2Q25 versus just 10.1% for Commercial Aviation). With significant backlog visibility, we can reasonably extrapolate growth in this segment out into the future, and as revenue mix gets higher, this should warrant ERJ to trade at a higher multiple.

Catalysts

One near-term catalyst is the possible removal of the 10% US import tariff. While management noted 2Q25 results were not materially impacted, my view is that the tariff remains an unnecessary headwind, particularly as ERJ looks to expand in the world’s largest aviation market. The angle here is that with the landmark Avelo order, the first E2 deal in the US, it gives relevant negotiating parties leverage in discussions, in that a domestic airline is already choosing ERJ’s product. If tariffs are rolled back, my expectation is that the impact would be immediate and high quality, as it flows directly to the bottom line and accelerates the US growth story.

Valuation

My new way of valuing ERJ is by comparing it against competitors (Boeing, Airbus, and Bombardier) in the same space, and via this lens, I still believe ERJ is undervalued by the market. The chart shows the expected EBITDA growth of these peers, and as you can see, ERJ has the highest growth outlook (17%), but despite that, ERJ trades at 10x forward EBITDA, which is the low end of where peers trade at. Putting Boeing aside, which is still in a turnaround mode, even Airbus, which has a similar EBITDA growth outlook, is trading at a 3x premium. I expect ERJ to trade closer to Airbus (probably still at a slight discount because Airbus has a larger scale) and at a premium vs. Bombardier (ERJ EBITDA growth outlook is 2x that of Bombardier).

Using consensus EBITDA estimates, which have been fairly in line with actual performance over the past 2 years, ERJ should deliver $6.26 billion EBITDA in FY26. Multiplying that by 11x, we get to a share price target of $88, or 55% upside.

Risk

The expected production ramp may face delay because of global supply chain bottlenecks. On the financial side, near-term margins remain pressured by the current 10% US tariff. Competitive intensity is another factor. Should large incumbents like Boeing and Airbus drive down prices to protect market share, it would impact ERJ directly.

Conclusion

In conclusion, I maintain a buy rating on ERJ, as I see a compelling risk/reward skew. With a record $29.7 billion backlog, robust order flow across all segments, and the Avelo order serving as a strategic US beachhead, ERJ’s fundamentals look stronger than what the market is pricing in. Valuation remains attractive at just 10x forward EBITDA, well below peers despite higher growth. Even accounting for risks like supply chain bottlenecks and tariffs, I believe ERJ is positioned for a re-rating, with my $88 price target implying ~55% upside from current levels.

data.title: Toyota: Hybrids Now, Solid-State And Autonomy Later - This Stock Is Undervalued
data.description:
Investment Thesis

For me, Toyota (NYSE:TM) is still a compelling investment opportunity despite 1Q26 results showcasing some extra pressure from tariffs. We also saw a bit of FX volatility.

The company’s scale is nearly 10 million vehicles annually, which gives it unmatched cost leverage. Across their vehicle lineup, we’re seeing strong dominance in hybrids with 93% of electrified sales last quarter coming from this segment. It provides steady cash flow and some market share defense since EV adoption still remains uneven due to a lagging global infrastructure. However, plug-in hybrids are offering an additional bridge, and Toyota’s deliberate slow pace in full battery EVs is supported by heavy investment in solid-state batteries.

In more recent news, the e-Palette autonomous platform introduces a new Mobility-as-a-Service revenue stream that I see having nice tailwinds heading into 2027. It should help diversify revenue away from cyclical passenger car sales. Net liquid assets for the company are above ¥14 trillion with a reaffirmed ¥95 dividend.

Telling 1Q26 results

Last quarter, in terms of sales, the company brought in some interesting numbers. Consolidated revenues rose roughly 3.5% year-over-year to ¥12.25 trillion. We saw higher vehicle sales and a bigger mix of electrified vehicles sold. However, operating income fell 10.9% to ¥1.17 trillion. Margins also compressed from 11.1% to 9.5% since US tariffs alone shaved roughly ¥450 billion from earnings. Net income for Toyota dropped about 37% to ¥841 billion, and income before tax fell by more than ¥620 billion. Management decided to cut full-year operating income guidance by roughly ¥600 billion. New guidance estimates are now ¥3.2 trillion, after citing the sustained impact of tariffs and foreign exchange headwinds, although the revenue outlook of ¥48.5 trillion was held steady. Even though we saw these pressures, Toyota maintained its planned dividend for investors at ¥95 per share. This tells me there is still some confidence in its balance sheet and cost-reduction efforts in the near term.

A slow EV rollout

If you think back, Toyota essentially invented the hybrid category with the introduction of the Prius in 1997, and the category still remains one of its strongest hands. Their overall hybrid lineup remains very strong, with the Prius, Corolla, RAV4 and Highlander, and Sienna bringing a fuel-efficient edge to cars used for both family and utility. The hybrid category has so far offered the company an edge in markets where infrastructure is lagging behind considerably; the hybrid car is somewhat of a technology bridge. It keeps Toyota front and center, still making them profitable while their EV sales slowly gain traction in these markets.

In 1Q26, Toyota sold 1.165 million hybrid electric vehicles (HEVs), up about 16.7% year over year. So HEVs are all the traction right now, we’re seeing a step up in hybrid volumes, which is doing most of the heavy lifting on mix and margins since EVs are going through a bit of turbulence amid tariffs. They now represent 93% of total electrified vehicle sales; this kind of volume is helping weaken the tariff impact a lot, as well as those FX headwinds. It also lends a hand in certain regions where infrastructure is still patchy, meaning the tech just isn't in place widely enough to support full EVs.

Moving on to plug-in hybrids (PHEV), they still remain fairly small in sales at just 47,000 units for the quarter. However, this segment is still outpacing most others. We saw growth here, with a surge of 38.8% year over year, and many consumers are finding these vehicles very useful in locations where they still want extended electric driving but also want the flexibility of a combustion engine. In these markets, there are often certain tax incentives that help those sales gain traction. I see this segment as something that allows Toyota to remain very relevant as EV adoption ramps up. Obviously, EVs are gaining traction across North America; however, traction across other parts of the globe is more uneven, and many are looking to make the switch, but not a complete switch. So PHEVs give them that nice niche market.

The numbers are also showing us that slow adoption, battery electric vehicles (BEVs) also hit 47,000 units sold last quarter; however, the segment is only up 10.4% since this time last year. So there's an extremely cautious rollout here from Toyota; they don’t want to push them out prematurely, while HEVs are still doing so well. However, they’re continuing to invest in solid-state batteries, which is where we should see a step up in charging speeds and energy densities as soon as 2027. For now, Toyota’s BEV volumes lag Tesla (TSLA), BYD, and Hyundai/Kia, but the strategy is deliberate: scale hybrids today and press BEVs harder as technology and demand mature.

Taking a chance on autonomy

The latest news out of the company is the new e-Palette futuristic shuttle. It gives investors a small window into how Toyota is envisioning its own role in a shift from vehicle ownership to having a mobility service. The new autonomous vehicle was just unveiled in Tokyo last week and is tipped to be a level 4 autonomous, fully electric multipurpose platform. Deployment for the shuttle is set to be FY27 with a retail price of around ¥29 million or roughly $196,000. Clients would, of course, include fleet operators and institutions. So that would include things like airports, stadiums, universities, and municipalities. It's set to be a modular space on wheels, which can be optioned out as a passenger shuttle, a retail shop, a delivery van, or even an emergency vehicle. This gives the vehicle an edge: traditional vehicles see cyclical passenger car sales, however, with a platform that can be optioned out for any business, we may see revenue come more evenly throughout the year. With fleet sales, there should be a higher ASP than mass market cars, as well as recurring revenue from service contracts, which come with things like maintenance and charging infrastructure. Now, if Toyota can lock in some kind of software ecosystem, we may see this platform become something similar to Tesla's with autonomous driving subscriptions or BYD’s fleet contracts.

Such a platform would also be tailored to both Japan's and broader Asia’s demographic needs. There is an aging population in Japan, with the median age now up to 49. Rural populations are also declining, so having an autonomous mobility vehicle for elderly residents has nice tailwinds. We’re also seeing massive urban congestion across Asia, where governments have hinted at the need to reduce road traffic in megacities. Now, most automakers in the same space, like BYD and Tesla, are betting on a very limited pathway, such as only full battery EVs. However, with Toyota, we have a much larger spread across multiple powertrains. HEVs are obviously the cash cow; they make up that 90% majority of electrified sales and help Toyota keep that market share in struggling infrastructure markets. So we shouldn't see a late EV rollout here as a bad thing. Before they can diversify that powertrain, they must have the revenue diversification to back it up.

Near-term pain, but Toyota can still gain traction

Toyota’s near-term profitability is clearly under pressure. On one side, we have US tariffs cutting an estimated ¥1.4 trillion from FY2026 earnings. Operating margins compressed to 6.6% from those double-digit levels just a year ago. However, on the flip side, if we look beyond the current macro headwinds, the company still has a few advantages that not many other global automakers can match. Annual production is now approaching 10 million vehicles. Just the sheer scale of Toyota gives it purchasing power and operational efficiency. They should still maintain lower unit costs, especially as it begins to ramp up BEV volumes. Even though Tesla and BYD may lead in pure EV penetration, Toyota’s ability to spread its R&D spending across hybrids, plug-in hybrids, BEVs, and even hydrogen fuel cells reduces near-term risk for me. It allows them to keep that pricing flexibility in key segments. We’re already seeing this flexibility evident in the hybrid portfolio; strong volumes are allowing Toyota to cushion tariff and FX shocks without fully passing costs onto consumers. Vehicle prices are remaining stable.

We’re getting a taste of this financial discipline and capital strength in the dividend stability. Despite the guidance cut, the company maintained its dividend at ¥95 per share for investors. Net liquid assets are above ¥14 trillion, so Toyota has ample balance sheet flexibility to continue investing aggressively in the etch we’re seeing. From solid-state batteries to autonomous mobility, all while still rewarding shareholders. Few automakers can boast both the liquidity and discipline to maintain shareholder returns while absorbing a ¥1 trillion-plus tariff hit.

Valuation

Toyota shares currently trade at valuations that look very attractive. Investors are paying materially less per unit of revenue than they would for most peers, despite Toyota’s massive scale and balance sheet strength. If we combine this with its consistent dividend yield of just over 3%, Toyota can provide investors with both value and income at a time when the market remains fixated on other higher-multiple EV names. I see hybrids anchoring near-term profitability and solid-state battery progress offering long-term upside; however, rollout will take quite some time. On the 3-month chart, Toyota has only recently begun to outperform the S&P 500, but still lags in returns on the 6-month chart. Year-to-date shares have stayed relatively flat.

What's Next?

Moving forward, we have to keep a close eye on the tariff mitigation path and also the fix impact. We should expect USD/JPY stability around ¥145. Some analysts have tipped that their dividend will be cut in the near term. However, the warning they give is somewhat misleading. As per Seeking Alpha the company has a 478% cash dividend payout ratio. But it only reflects temporary tariff and FX-driven profit compression and is not a structural cash flow problem. Toyota holds over ¥14 trillion in net liquid assets and has always had a long history of maintaining payouts even in downturns. In reality, I think its dividend is well-covered and far safer than the quant model suggests.
